title: Graph labels and scales
description: Given the graph of a real-world relationship, determine whether the labels and scales of the graph's axes are appropriate.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dan went on a drive. He modeled the relationship between the distance he drives $D$ (in kilometers) and the remaining fuel  in his tank $F$ (in liters) as $F=40-0.1D$.

He wanted to graph the relationship for the entire distance of his drive, which was $100$ kilometers. Here is his work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Dan make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in?
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $300$, but Dan only wanted to graph the relationship for the first $100$ kilometers. So the scale isn't appropriate. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis goes from $0$ to $50$, which is appropriate because it allows us to see the entire relationship. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Distance (kilometers)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Fuel (liters)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

In conclusion, this is the mistake Dan made when drawing the graph:

- Inappropriate scale for $x$-axis

options.alt: A line decreasing as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by fifty from zero to three hundred. The y axis scales by five from zero to forty five. . The y axis is labeled Fuel in liters. The x axis is labeled Distance in kilometers. The line starts at the y axis at (zero, forty) passing through the point (three hundred, ten) before reaching the part of the x axis it would touch.

assessment_items.question_content:
Emilia watched a hovercraft take off from an elevated platform. She modeled the relationship between the time $T$ since takeoff (in seconds) and the hovercraft's elevation $E$ above the ground (in meters) as $E=(11-T)(T+3)$.

She wanted to create a graph to show the hovercraft's motion until it landed on the ground. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Emilia make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in?
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $-10$ to $40$, which is inappropriate because negative input values don't make sense, and because the hovercraft landed after about $11$ seconds, so no need to go until $40$. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis goes from $0$ to $50$, which is appropriate because it allows us to see the entire motion of the hovercraft, from the highest point to the lowest point (which is when it landed). | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis doesn't have any label, which isn't good! It should have a label like "Time (seconds)". | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Elevation (centimeters)" but the units Emilia used are meters, not centimeters. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

In conclusion, these are the mistakes Emilia made when drawing the graph:

- Inappropriate scale for $x$-axis
- Wrong/missing label for $x$-axis
- Wrong/missing label for $y$-axis

options.alt: A concave down parabola on an x y coordinate plane. The x and y axes scale by five. The y axis scales from zero to fifty. The x axis scales from negative ten to forty. The y axis is labeled Elevation in centimeters. The parabola passes through thirty two and one half on the y axis with a maximum around (four, fifty nine). It touches the x axis around negative three and eleven.

assessment_items.question_content:
Benjamin invests $\$400$ in a savings account that earns $5\%$ interest each year. He models the relationship between the time $T$ since he opened the account (in years) and the account's balance $B$ (in dollars) as $B=400\cdot1.05^T$.

He wanted to create a graph to show the account's balance over the first $10$ years. Here is his work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Benjamin make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in?
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $8$, but Benjamin wanted to graph the relationship for the first $10$ years, so the scale for $x$-axis is inappropriate. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis goes from $-600$ to $600$, but the balance is never negative, so there's no need to include negative $y$-values. This scale is inappropriate. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Time (years)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Balance (dollars)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

In conclusion, these are the mistakes Benjamin made when drawing the graph:

- Inappropriate scale for $x$-axis
- Inappropriate scale for $y$-axis

options.alt: A line increasing as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by one from zero to eight. The y axis scales by one hundred from negatve six hundred to six hundred. The y axis is labeled Balance in dollars. The x axis is labeled time in years. The line starts at the y axis at (zero, four hundred) passing through the point (four and one half, five hundred) before reaching around (eight, six hundred).

assessment_items.question_content:
Lauren modeled the relationship between the number of days $T$ that had passed since she planted a cactus and its height $H$ (in centimeters) as $H=10+2T$.

She wanted to create a graph to show the cactus's growth in the first month. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Lauren make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Lauren wanted to model the growth over the first month.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $30$, which is appropriate because Lauren wanted to model the growth for the first month. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis goes from $0$ to $30$, which is inappropriate because we can't see the height after more than $12$ days or so. The range for the $y$-axis should be longer. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Days since planting," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis doesn't have any label, which isn't good! It should be labeled as "Height (cm)". | $\redD{\text{No}}$

In conclusion, these are the mistakes Lauren made when drawing the graph:

- Inappropriate scale for $y$-axis
- Wrong/missing label for $y$-axis

options.alt: A line increases as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x and y axes scales by five from zero to thirty. The x axis is labeled Days since planting. The line starts at the y axis at (zero, ten) passing through the point (five, twenty) before reaching the point (ten, thirty).

assessment_items.question_content:
Catalina opened a new restaurant. She modeled the relationship between the number of months $T$ since she opened the restaurant and its profit $P$ (in dollars, measured as income minus expenses) as $P=80(T-4)^2-300$.

She wanted to create a graph to show the restaurant's profits over the first year. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Catalina make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Lauren wanted to model the growth over the first month.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $12$, which is appropriate because Catalina wanted to model the profit for the first year. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis goes from $0$ to $6000$, which is inappropriate because we can't see the graph when the profit is negative. The range for the $y$-axis should include negative values as well. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Months," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Profit (dollars)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$

In conclusion, this is the mistake Catalina made when drawing the graph:

- Inappropriate scale for $y$-axis

options.alt: A concave up parabola on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by one from zero to thirteen. The y axis scales by five hundred from zero to six thousand. The y axis is labeled Profit in dollars. The x axis is labeled months. The parabola touches the y axis at (zero, one thousand) and touches the x axis around (two, zero). The vertex in the negative y axis but is not displayed on the graph. It touches the x axis again at (six, zero) and goes up through the point (eight, one thousand).

assessment_items.question_content:
Clemence studies a sample of a chemical element called Einsteinium-253, which naturally loses its mass over time. She modeled the relationship between the time $T$ since she started studying the sample (in days) and the sample's mass $M$ (in grams) as $M=600\cdot0.5^{^{\scriptsize\dfrac{T}{20.5}}}$.

She wanted to create a graph to show the sample's decay in the first month. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Clemence make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Lauren wanted to model the growth over the first month.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $30$, which is appropriate because Clemence wanted to model the relationship for the first month. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis goes from $0$ to $2000$, but the mass's highest value is $600$ grams (at $T=0$). The range is too large, which makes the scale inappropriate. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Mass (grams)" but that's the label of the $y$-axis. The $x$-axis label should be "Time (days)". | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Time (days)" but that's the label of the $x$-axis. The $y$-axis label should be "Mass (grams)". | $\redD{\text{No}}$

In conclusion, these are the mistakes Clemence made when drawing the graph:

- Inappropriate scale for $y$-axis
- Wrong/missing label for $x$-axis
- Wrong/missing label for $y$-axis

options.alt: An exponential function decreases as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by three from zero to thirty. The y axis scales by two hundred from zero to two thousand. The x axis is labeled Mass in grams. The y axis is labeled Time in days. The graph starts at the y axis at (zero, six hundred) passing through the points around (twelve, four hundred) and (thirty, two hundred).

assessment_items.question_content:
Sean drank a slushy as fast as he could. He modeled the relationship between the time since he started drinking $T$ (in seconds) and the remaining amount of slushy $S$ (in milliliters) as $S=600-7.5T$.

He wanted to graph the relationship from the moment he started drinking until he finished the slushy. Here is his work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Sean make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in?
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $160$, but Sean finished the slushy after $80$ seconds. So the scale isn't appropriate. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis goes from $0$ to $2000$, but the initial amount of slushy (which is the maximum value) is $600$ milliliters. So the scale isn't appropriate. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Time (seconds)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Slushy (liters)" but the units Sean used are milliliters, not liters. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

In conclusion, these are the mistakes Sean made when drawing the graph:

- Inappropriate scale for $x$-axis
- Inappropriate scale for $y$-axis
- Wrong/missing label for $y$-axis

options.alt: A line decreasing as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by twenty from zero to one hundred sixty. The y axis scales by two hundred. The y axis is labeled slushy in liters. The x axis is labeled time in seconds. The line starts at the y axis at (zero, six hundred) passing through the point (forty, ten) before reaching the x axis at (eighty, zero).

assessment_items.question_content:
Ciara modeled the relationship between the power generated by an electrical circuit $P$ (in watts) and the circuit's current $C$ (in amperes) as $P=-15C(C-8)$.

She wanted to graph the relationship as the current increases from $0$ until the power is $0$. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Ciara make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Lauren wanted to model the growth over the first month.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $8$, which is appropriate because it starts when the current is $0$ and ends when the power is $0$. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis is appropriate because it allows us to see the relationship's behavior throughout the entire range. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Current (watts)" but the units for current are amperes, not watts. | $\redD{\text{No}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Power (amperes)" but the units for power are watts, not amperes. | $\redD{\text{No}}$

In conclusion, these are the mistakes Ciara made when drawing the graph:

- Wrong/missing label for $x$-axis
- Wrong/missing label for $y$-axis

options.alt: A concave down parabola on an x y coordinate plane. The y axis scales by twenty five from zero to two hundred fifty. The x axis scales by one from zero to eight. The y axis is labeled Power in amperes. The x axis is labeled current in watts. The parabola starts at (zero, zero). It increases up to a maximum around (four, two hundred thirty seven) before decreasing and touching the x axis at (eight, zero).

assessment_items.question_content:
Matias models the relationship between Australia's gross domestic product per capita $P$ (in dollars) and time $T$ (in years after 1950) as $P=1800\cdot 1.067^T$.

He wanted to create a graph to show the relationship until the year 2000. Here is his work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Matias make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Lauren wanted to model the growth over the first month.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $50$, which is appropriate because Matias wanted to model the relationship until 2000, which is $50$ years after 1950. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis is appropriate because it allows us to see the relationship's behavior throughout the entire range. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Years since 1950," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis doesn't have any label, which isn't good! It should be labeled as "Gross domestic product per capita (dollars)". | $\redD{\text{No}}$

In conclusion, this is the mistake Matias made when drawing the graph:

- Wrong/missing label for $y$-axis

options.alt: An exponential function increases as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by five from zero to fifty. The y axis scales by five thousand from zero to forty five thousand. The x axis is labeled Years since 1950. The graph starts at the y axis at (zero, one thousand eight hundred) passing through the points around (fifteen, five thousand) and (fifty, forty five thousand).

assessment_items.question_content:
Isabella filled a pool with water, and modeled the relationship between the time since she started filling the pool $T$ (in minutes) and the amount of water in the pool $W$ (in liters) as $W=135T$.

She wanted to graph the relationship until the pool is full, which is when there are $16{,}000$ liters of water. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Isabella make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Lauren wanted to model the growth over the first month.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $120$, which is appropriate because Isabella wanted to graph the relationship until the pool is full, and it seems the pool filled up after about $120$ minutes. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis is appropriate because it allows us to see the relationship's behavior throughout the entire range. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Time (minutes)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Water (liters)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
In conclusion, Isabella didn't make any mistake.

options.alt: A line increasing as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by twelve from zero to one hundred twenty. The y axis scales by two thousand from zero to eighteen thousand. The y axis is labeled Water in liters. The x axis is labeled time in minutes. The line starts at the y axis at (zero, zero) passing through the point around (sixty, eight thousand) before reaching around (one hundred twenty, sixteen thousand).

assessment_items.question_content:
Jasmin modeled the relationship between the altitude of a tree $A$ (in meters above sea level) and the tree's growth rate $R$ (in millimeters per year) as $R=-0.03A^2+0.42A+1.13$.

She wanted to graph the relationship for altitudes between $0$ and $9000$ meters. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Jasmin make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Jasmin wanted to model the altitudes between $0$ and $9000$ meters.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the set of altitudes we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $9000$, which is appropriate because Jasmin wanted to graph the relationship for altitudes between $0$ and $9000$ meters. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis is appropriate because it allows us to see the relationship's behavior throughout the entire range. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Altitude (meters above sea level)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Growth rate (millimeters per year)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
In conclusion, Jasmin didn't make any mistake.

options.alt: A concave down parabola on an x y coordinate plane. The y axis scales by four tenths from zero to two and eight tenths. The x axis scales by one thousand from zero to nine thousand. The y axis is labeled Growth rate in millimeters per year. The x axis is labeled altitude in meters above sea level. The parabola starts at (zero, one and thirteen hundredths). It increases up to a maximum around (seven thousand, two and six tenths) before decreasing.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ashlynn models the relationship between the temperature $C$ of a hot cup of tea (in degrees Celsius) and time $T$ since the tea was poured into the cup (in minutes) as $C=20+20\cdot 0.88^T$.

She wanted to graph the relationship over the first half hour. Here is her work:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What mistakes did Ashlynn make when drawing the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Here are the questions we should ask ourselves in order to determine whether the graph is appropriate or not:

Feature | Question
:- | :-
$x$-axis scale | Does the $x$-axis cover all the values we are interested in? Does it cover values we are not interested in? Remember that Lauren wanted to model the growth over the first month.
$y$-axis scale | Is the $y$-axis scaled in a way that allows us to see the entire behavior of the graph for the duration we are interested in?
Axis labels | Do the axes have labels, and if so, do the labels describe the appropriate variable and provide the appropriate unit for that variable?

And here is our analysis of all those features:

Feature | Analysis | Appropriate?
:- | :- | :-
$x$-axis scale | The $x$-axis goes from $0$ to $30$, which is appropriate because Ashlynn wanted to graph the relationship for the first half hour, which is $30$ minutes. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis scale | The $y$-axis is appropriate because it allows us to see the relationship's behavior throughout the entire range. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$x$-axis label | The $x$-axis is labeled as "Time (minutes)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
$y$-axis label | The $y$-axis is labeled as "Temperature (degrees Celsius)," which is indeed an appropriate label for it. | $\greenD{\text{Yes}}$
In conclusion, Ashlynn didn't make any mistake.

options.alt: An exponential function decreases as x increases on an x y coordinate plane. The x axis scales by five from zero to thirty. The y axis scales by five from zero to forty five. The x axis is labeled Time in minutes. The y axis is labeled Temperature in degrees Celsius. The graph starts at the y axis at (zero, forty) passing through the points around (five, four thirty) and (ten, twenty five). The asymptote is at y equals twenty.
